title: Weakly interacting Bose gases in the canonical ensemble
authors: Jonata S. Soares, Axel Pelster, Arnaldo Gammal
abstract: Based on the canonical description of a non-interacting Bose gas, we work out how both thermodynamic and statistical properties change perturbatively with respect to weak two-particle interactions. Up to first order, we obtain a recursion formula for the canonical partition function, which consists of the same Feynman diagrams as the grand-canonical description but with different Feynman rules. Resumming this recursion formula for the canonical partition function allows one to characterize the statistics of the ground-state occupancy by its respective cumulants. We demonstrate the applicability of this approach by analyzing a dilute Bose gas with contact interaction in a box trap. To this end, we used Dirichlet boundary conditions in view of their relevance for current experiments with atomic gases, where the box trap is implemented, for instance, with digital mirror devices.
summary: This paper develops a perturbative quantum field theory approach to calculate the thermodynamic and statistical properties of weakly interacting Bose gases within the canonical ensemble. By deriving resummed recursion formulas for the partition function, it accurately predicts observables like condensate occupancy cumulants and critical temperature shifts, particularly useful for understanding finite-size effects in trapped atomic systems.
main_problem: To study how thermodynamic and statistical properties of weakly interacting Bose gases change perturbatively with respect to weak two-particle interactions in the canonical ensemble.
main_result: The authors derive resummed recursion formulas for the canonical partition function and calculate the cumulants of ground-state occupancy, providing quantitative predictions for finite systems.
method: The calculation employs path integrals and perturbation theory, developing a recursive approach to handle the interacting partition function in the canonical ensemble.
model_or_system: The system is a weakly interacting Bose gas confined in a box trap geometry, analyzed using Dirichlet boundary conditions relevant for current atomic gas experiments. Interactions are treated perturbatively via contact potentials.
key_observables: Canonical partition function ($Z_N( eta)$), one-particle density matrix ($ho^{(0)}_N$), ground-state occupancy probability distribution $p^{(0)}_q(k|N,  eta)$, and cumulants ($\kappa_1$ to $\kappa_4$).
important_parameters: Weak interaction strength (perturbation order), temperature ($T$) relative to critical temperatures ($T_c$), and particle number ($N$).
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to first-order perturbation theory in the weak interaction potential, which leads to known limitations compared to non-perturbative results like Bogoliubov theory at zero temperature.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate Feynman diagrams for Hartree and Fock energies, show plots of the first four cumulants ($\kappa_1$ to $\kappa_4$) versus $T/T_c^{(0)}$ comparing ideal vs. interacting cases, and plot the critical temperature shift ($\Delta T_c/T_c$) derived from heat capacity and fluctuation measurements.
paper_structure: The paper builds from deriving non-interacting results via cycle decomposition to establishing recursive relations for $Z_N( eta)$. It then calculates first-order corrections involving Hartree and Fock terms, culminating in the resummation of these formulas to determine cumulants and thermodynamic properties like heat capacity.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous theoretical framework for calculating finite-size effects and interaction corrections (beyond mean-field) in Bose gases using the canonical ensemble, which is crucial for interpreting modern trapped-atom experiments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Example of two interacting cycles of length k = 3: Direct path (black) of Eq. (45) has the same initial and final interaction point, whereas exchange path (blue) of Eq. (46) has different initial and final interaction points.

FIG. 2. Feynman cylinder representation of the Hartree and Fock energies for three particles corresponding to Eqs. (48) and (49). The Hartree energy corresponds to two closed cycles of lengths 1 and 2, between which an interaction occurs, whereas the Fock energy represents one closed cycle of length 3, where an open cycle of length 1 interacts within another open cycle of length 2.

FIG. 3. Feynman diagrams for canonical Hartree and Fock energies involving three particles. Each line corresponds to an imaginary-time propagator, where the attached number indicates the number of windings around the Feynman cylinder depicted in Fig. 2.

FIG. 4. Triangle for nested summation in (n, k) plane. In Eq. (58) the left-hand side corresponds to adding points following each vertical line and later summing all vertical lines, whereas the sum at right-hand side adds all points along each horizontal line and later sums over all the horizontal lines.

FIG. 5. First four cumulants from Eqs. (75)–(78) for 500 particles as function of dimensionless temperature T/T (0) c with the grand-canonical critical temperature T (0) c = (2πℏ2/MkB)[N/L3ζ(3/2)]2/3. Dashed lines are for the non-interacting case, i.e. an1/3 = 0, and solid lines for the weakly interacting case an1/3 = 0.1 [44].
